--- SOURCE 2: Photonic Crystal - an overview ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/photonic-crystal

SUMMARY:
The photonic crystals (also known as photonic band-gap materials) are a new type of materials in which the periodic dielectric structures with a band gap forbid propagation of a certain frequency range of light. Photonic crystals (PCs) are highly ordered materials that possess a periodically modulated dielectric constant, with the properties of confining and controlling the propagation of light owing to the existence of photonic band gap, a band of frequencies in which light propagation in the photonic crystal is forbidden (Freymann et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2013; Koenderink and Vos, 2005). Photonic crystals (PCs) having a three-dimensional (3-D) ordered structure with a periodicity of optical wavelength have attracted considerable attention from both fundamental and practical points of view, because in such materials novelphysical concepts such as photonic band gap have been theoretically predicted and various applications of photonic crystals have been proposed [1, 2].

### 3 Summary and Future Prospects

Recent advances in [photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/photonics "Learn more about photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") crystals have been discussed in terms of band-gap/defect engineering. Although many of the explanations given may have been insufficient due to space limitations, it is hoped that the reader gets a sense of the steady advances made in the last few years in fundamental technology for operating and controlling light in extremely small volumes, which was the initial expectation of photonic crystals.

It is expected that, over the next ten years, nanoprocessing technology will dramatically advance and that more reliable and precise devices will continue to be developed. In the case of 2D [photonic crystal](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-crystal "Learn more about photonic crystal from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") slabs, there is promise of remarkable advances in Si-based systems, together with progress in integration with [electronic circuits](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/networks-circuits "Learn more about electronic circuits from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Further advances can be expected in combined optical and electronic circuits equipped with features such as [optical switching](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/optical-switching "Learn more about optical switching from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), tuning, and delay functionality. It is expected that the main components of such circuits will be optical, and optical/electronic chips will be developed. There is no doubt that the size and [power consumption](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electric-power-utilization "Learn more about power consumption from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of such devices will be more than tens to hundreds of times smaller than they are now. Advances in a large number of applications can also be expected, including developments in next-generation miniaturized multiwavelength [light sources](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/light-source "Learn more about light sources from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") based on the addition of active functionality, supersensitive sensors, optical memory functions that require a high-_Q_ value, and single-photon light sources.

### I Introduction

Electromagnetism is the fundamental mediator of all interactions in [atomic physics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/atomic-physics "Learn more about atomic physics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [condensed matter physics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/condensed-matter-physics "Learn more about condensed matter physics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), in other words, the force that governs the structure of ordinary matter. In a novel class of engineered dielectric materials known as [photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/photonics "Learn more about photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") bandgap (PBG) materials, a fundamentally new [electromagnetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/electromagnetic "Learn more about electromagnetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") effect can be realized. This phenomenon is the localization of light (John, 1984; Anderson, 1985) and it may prove central to the utilization of light waves for [information and communication technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/information-and-communication-technology "Learn more about information and communication technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In the 19th century, James Clerk Maxwell deduced a precise and elegant [mathematical description](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mathematical-description "Learn more about mathematical description from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the propagation of light. Maxwell's theory of electromagnetic [wave propagation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/wave-propagation "Learn more about wave propagation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") was shortly thereafter tested and verified by Heinrich Hertz. There began the age of [wireless communication](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wireless-communication "Learn more about wireless communication from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This allowed ships at sea to communicate with land. Today, the same basic discovery provides us with the use of radio, television, and mobile phones. Despite the widespread knowledge and use of Maxwell's theory of electromagnetism, it has only recently been recognized that light waves not only propagate, they can also be trapped.

The invention of the laser in the latter half of the 20th century was another milestone in the science and technology of light. Laser light allows us to probe the structure of matter with unprecedented accuracy, it provides medical practitioners with a cutting tool sharper than any surgeon's knife, and allows us to modulate communication signals for high-speed [data transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/data-transfer "Learn more about data transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") along the Internet. Today, using laser light, undersea [fiber optic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fiber-optics "Learn more about fiber optic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") cables carry enormous amounts of voice communications and data with such clarity that a pin drop in Karlsruhe, [Germany](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/germany "Learn more about Germany from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), can be heard clearly in Toronto, Canada.

[Optical fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-fiber "Learn more about Optical fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are replacing electrical wires in shorter distance communications such as local access networks and computer-to-computer communications. In a completely seamless network, communications between nearby computer chips and even within a single computer chip would take place with tiny beams of laser light rather than electricity. Optical computers of this type may be faster and support neural architectures (circuit interconnections resembling that of a human brain), unlike their electronic counterparts, which are restricted in architecture due to electrical cross-talk between nearby wires. That is to say, electrical current in one wire can disturb electrical signals passing through nearby wires. [Laser beams](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/laser-beams "Learn more about Laser beams from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), on the other hand, can coexist without disrupting one another.

The trapping and micromolding of light flow needed for the applications suggested above requires materials which can scatter light much more strongly than any naturally occurring material. We experience multiple light scattering when it becomes dark on a cloudy day. Light from the sun scatters many times from water droplets, following a tortuous [diffusion path](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/diffusion-path "Learn more about diffusion path from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") before reaching the ground. The distance the light travels within the cloud before it is scattered into a random direction is called the [mean free path](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/mean-free-path "Learn more about mean free path from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The effect of multiple scattering is that the amount of light transmitted through the cloud is reduced by a factor of the ratio of the cloud thickness to the mean free path. The rest comes back out the other side, which is why clouds appear white. Multiple light scattering also takes place in human tissue. Here the transport mean free path for light of 1 μm wavelength is about 1 mm.

Neither clouds nor human tissue can scatter light sufficiently strongly to localize light. For this to happen a collection of microscopic dielectric structures that scatter light 1000 times more strongly than human tissue is required. In this case the transport mean free path becomes as short as the wavelength of light itself. If, in addition to the strong resonant scattering of the individual dielectric particles, there is a periodic arrangement of the [scatterers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/scattering "Learn more about scatterers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), then pathways for [light propagation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/light-propagation "Learn more about light propagation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") over specific frequency intervals can be completely removed. The removal of pathways over all directions over a band of frequencies is referred to as the creation of a photonic bandgap (PBG). Dielectric microstructures that exhibit this effect are called [PBG materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/photonic-crystal "Learn more about PBG materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

In electronic microcircuits, electrical currents are guided by thin metal wires. Electrons are bound within the [cross section](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/cross-section "Learn more about cross section from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") of the wire by the so-called [work function](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/work-function "Learn more about work function from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (confining potential) of the metal. As a result, electrical currents follow the path prescribed by the wire without escaping into the background. The situation is very different for [optical waves](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-wave "Learn more about optical waves from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Although [optical fibers](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/fiber-optics "Learn more about optical fibers from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") guide light over long distances, microcircuits of light based on fibers do not exist. This is because empty space is already an ideal conductor of light waves. The light in an [optical fiber](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/optical-fiber "Learn more about optical fiber from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can easily escape into the background electromagnetic modes of empty space if the fiber is bent or distorted on a [microscopic scale](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/microscopic-scale "Learn more about microscopic scale from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). PBG materials remove this problem by removing all the background electromagnetic modes over the relevant band of frequencies. Light paths can be created inside a [PBG material](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/photonic-crystal "Learn more about PBG material from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the form of engineered [waveguide channels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/channel-waveguide "Learn more about waveguide channels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The PBG localizes the light and prevents it from escaping the optical microcircuit.

The question of whether light can be localized can be posed in the form of an analogy between [Maxwell's equations](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/maxwell-equation "Learn more about Maxwell's equations from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [electromagnetic wave](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/electromagnetic-radiation "Learn more about electromagnetic wave from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") propagation and Schrödinger's equation for electrons propagating in a scattering potential. Consider a monochromatic electromagnetic wave of [frequency ω](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/frequency-omega "Learn more about frequency ω from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") propagating in a medium whose dielectric constant varies from point to point in space as

(1)ϵ x=ϵ 0+ϵ fluct x.

Here, ε 0 is the average part of the dielectric constant and ε fluct(_x_) represents the part of the dielectric constant that varies from point to point in space. We assume that the dielectric microstructure does not absorb the light and that the total dielectric constant is everywhere real and positive. The wave equation for such an [optical field](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-field "Learn more about optical field from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is given by

(2)−∇2 E→+∇→(∇→⋅E→)−ω 2 c 2 ɛ fluct(x)E→=ω 2 c 2 ɛ 0 E→.

Equation (2) has been written in a form which resembles the quantum mechanical Schrödinger equation. Here, the first two terms are the analogs of the “kinetic energy” terms in Schrödinger's equation, (ω 2/_c_ 2)ε fluct(_x_) plays the role of a scattering potential, and (ω 2/_c_ 2)ε 0 is analogous to an energy eigenvalue. The Maxwell wave equation (2) describes a vector wave field as opposed to complex scalar wavefunction in Schrödinger's equation.

The essential subtlety of light localization is apparent from Eq. (2). Unlike electrons, which can be trapped and localized for negative eigenvalues (bound states) in corresponding negative-energy potential wells, the overall positivity of the dielectric constant (1) leads to the constraint that the energy eigenvalue, (ω 2/_c_ 2)ε 0 is always greater than the highest of the potential barriers presented by (ω 2/_c_ 2)ε fluct(x). The occurrence of bound states of the electromagnetic wave field in this [spectral range](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/spectral-range "Learn more about spectral range from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") requires extremely specialized and carefully engineered dielectric materials.

### 1.1 What Is a Photonic Crystal

[Photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/photonics "Learn more about Photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") crystal (PhC) structures have long been in the spotlight of laser research, due to their enticing characteristics that can enable extreme tailoring of the [electromagnetic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/electromagnetic "Learn more about electromagnetic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (EM) field, applicable for the thorough control of the spontaneous emission of a laser. The term “photonic crystal” was initially introduced during the 1980s by Yablonovitch (1987) and John (1987). Yablonovitch's work revolved around the inhibition of spontaneous emission in a wavelength-scale periodic [refractive index](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/refractive-index "Learn more about refractive index from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") modulation in space (Yablonovitch, 1987), while John worked on its acceleration (John, 1987). The [PhC](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-crystal "Learn more about PhC from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") structures were inspired by atomic lattices, having spatially varying dielectric constant, as atomic lattices have the atomic potential. Thus, a significant feature of PhC structures is the [photonic band gap](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-band-gap "Learn more about photonic band gap from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (PBG), where propagation of light is blocked for specific wavelength bands.

It comes without doubt that one of the primary targets when developing lasers is the absolute minimization of the lasing threshold. To achieve that, of course, resonating structures of high quality are needed. PhCs are among the best in this category, as they exhibit record _Q_/_V_ ratios, where _Q_ is the quality factor and _V_ the modal volume of the resonant mode.

This chapter is dedicated to PhC nanolasers, and particularly those integrated on [silicon](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silicon "Learn more about silicon from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (Si) boards. Until recently, PhC nanolasers were at an infant level, having still several issues to overcome before making the next step to become a force to be reckoned in the commercial world. However, lately significant progress has been made, indicating perhaps that it is the right time for PhC nanolasers to make this next step. In this chapter, we revisit the roadmap that led to this evolution and analyze the main milestones behind it.

This chapter is divided into four parts:

The first one deals with the main guidelines that need to be taken into account when designing a nanocavity and the issues that need to be overcome to develop high-performance and efficient PhC nanolasers. In the second part, we will analyze the design and performance of optically pumped PhC nanolasers on [Si](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silicon "Learn more about Si from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or silicon-on-insulator (SOI) wafers and, in specific, PhC nanolasers on blank Si—no waveguides—and [SOI](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/silicon "Learn more about SOI from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") circuitry, which can serve as the basis for the solving of several issues. The third part will review the latest research efforts on electrically injected PhC nanolasers on Si/SOI circuitry, which have solved all the major issues and promise to bring PhC nanolasers on the next crest of the wave of commercial CMOS integrated [nanophotonics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/nanophotonics "Learn more about nanophotonics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

Finally, the fourth part will summarize and provide examples for further manipulation of PhC nanocavities in alternative structures.

### 5.01.2.1.4 Photonic crystals

[Photonic crystals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-crystal "Learn more about Photonic crystals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are [macroporous](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/macro-porosity "Learn more about macroporous from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") materials with interesting properties, especially [optical properties](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/optical-property "Learn more about optical properties from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").7 They are regular arrangements of matter on the scale corresponding roughly to the wavelength of [visible light](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/physics-and-astronomy/visible-spectrum "Learn more about visible light from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), that is, on the order of several 100 nm. Due to the fact that [photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/photonics "Learn more about photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") crystals are periodic on the same length scale as light, they influence the propagation of light. [XRD](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/ray-diffraction "Learn more about XRD from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") does not provide substantial information on the structure of photonic crystals. However, [neutron diffraction](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/neutron-diffraction "Learn more about neutron diffraction from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is a suitable technique. Further, light diffraction could be used for the analysis of photonic crystals. However, as visible light and the photonic crystal interact strongly with each other, multiple scattering occurs which makes [structure elucidation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemistry/structure-elucidation "Learn more about structure elucidation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") very difficult.

### 12.4.1 The concept of photonic crystals

Photonic crystals (PCs) are highly ordered materials that possess a periodically modulated dielectric constant, with the properties of confining and controlling the propagation of light owing to the existence of [photonic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/photonics "Learn more about photonic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") band gap, a band of frequencies in which [light propagation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/light-propagation "Learn more about light propagation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") in the [photonic crystal](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-crystal "Learn more about photonic crystal from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") is forbidden (Freymann et al., 2013; Lee et al., 2013; [Koenderink](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/koenderink "Learn more about Koenderink from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and Vos, 2005). Therefore, [photonic crystals](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/photonic-crystal "Learn more about photonic crystals from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are also known as photonic [band gap materials](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/band-gap-material "Learn more about band gap materials from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Photonic crystals have been the subject of numerous investigations since the original work of Yablonoviteh (1987) and John (1987). Because of their unique characteristics, the potential applications of photonic crystals are highly prospective, ranging from gas sensing to [optical filters](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/optical-filter "Learn more about optical filters from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), photonic papers, inkless printing, and reflective flat displays.

# Photonic crystal

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/03/Opal_Armband_800pix.jpg/250px-Opal_Armband_800pix.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Teinopalpus_imperialis_verso.JPG/250px-Teinopalpus_imperialis_verso.JPG)

A **photonic crystal** is an [optical](/wiki/Optical "Optical") [nanostructure](/wiki/Nanostructure "Nanostructure") in which the [refractive index](/wiki/Refractive_index "Refractive index") changes periodically. This affects the propagation of light in the same way that the structure of [natural crystals](/wiki/Crystal_structure "Crystal structure") gives rise to [X-ray diffraction](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography") and that the atomic lattices (crystal structure) of [semiconductors](/wiki/Semiconductors "Semiconductors") affect their conductivity of [electrons](/wiki/Electron "Electron"). Photonic crystals occur in nature in the form of [structural coloration](/wiki/Structural_coloration "Structural coloration") and [animal reflectors](/wiki/Animal_reflectors "Animal reflectors"), and, as artificially produced, promise to be useful in a range of applications.

Photonic crystals can be fabricated for one, two, or three dimensions. One-dimensional photonic crystals can be made of [thin film](/wiki/Thin_film "Thin film") layers deposited on each other. Two-dimensional ones can be made by [photolithography](/wiki/Photolithography "Photolithography"), or by drilling holes in a suitable substrate. Fabrication methods for three-dimensional ones include drilling under different angles, stacking multiple 2-D layers on top of each other, [direct laser writing](/wiki/Direct_laser_writing "Direct laser writing"), or, for example, instigating self-assembly of spheres in a matrix and dissolving the spheres.

Photonic crystals can, in principle, find uses wherever light must be manipulated. For example, [dielectric mirrors](/wiki/Dielectric_mirrors "Dielectric mirrors") are one-dimensional photonic crystals which can produce ultra-high reflectivity mirrors at a specified wavelength. Two-dimensional photonic crystals called [photonic-crystal fibers](/wiki/Photonic-crystal_fiber "Photonic-crystal fiber") are used for [fiber-optic communication](/wiki/Fiber-optic_communication "Fiber-optic communication"), among other applications. Three-dimensional crystals may one day be used in [optical computers](/wiki/Optical_computer "Optical computer"), and could lead to more efficient [photovoltaic cells](/wiki/Photovoltaic_cell "Photovoltaic cell").[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Although the energy of light (and all [electromagnetic radiation](/wiki/Electromagnetic_radiation "Electromagnetic radiation")) is quantized in units called [photons](/wiki/Photon "Photon"), the analysis of photonic crystals requires only [classical physics](/wiki/Classical_physics "Classical physics"). "Photonic" in the name is a reference to [photonics](/wiki/Photonics "Photonics"), a modern designation for the study of light ([optics](/wiki/Optics "Optics")) and optical engineering. Indeed, the first research into what we now call photonic crystals may have been as early as 1887 when the English physicist [Lord Rayleigh](/wiki/Lord_Rayleigh "Lord Rayleigh") experimented with periodic multi-layer [dielectric](/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric") stacks, showing they can effect a photonic [band-gap](/wiki/Band-gap "Band-gap") in one dimension. Research interest grew with work in 1987 by [Eli Yablonovitch](/wiki/Eli_Yablonovitch "Eli Yablonovitch") and [Sajeev John](/wiki/Sajeev_John "Sajeev John") on periodic optical structures with more than one dimension—now called photonic crystals.

## Introduction

Photonic crystals are composed of periodic [dielectric](/wiki/Dielectric "Dielectric"), metallo-dielectric—or even [superconductor](/wiki/Superconductor "Superconductor") microstructures or [nanostructures](/wiki/Nanostructure "Nanostructure") that affect [electromagnetic wave](/wiki/Electromagnetic_waves "Electromagnetic waves") propagation in the same way that the [periodic potential](/wiki/Bloch_wave "Bloch wave") in a [semiconductor](/wiki/Semiconductor "Semiconductor") crystal affects the propagation of [electrons](/wiki/Electrons "Electrons"), determining allowed and forbidden electronic [energy bands](/wiki/Energy_bands "Energy bands"). Photonic crystals contain regularly repeating regions of high and low [refractive index](/wiki/Refractive_index "Refractive index"). Light waves may propagate through this structure or propagation may be disallowed, depending on their wavelength. Wavelengths that may propagate in a given direction are called *[modes](/wiki/Normal_modes "Normal modes")*, and the ranges of wavelengths which propagate are called *bands*. Disallowed bands of [wavelengths](/wiki/Wavelength "Wavelength") are called *photonic [band gaps](/wiki/Band_gap "Band gap")*. This gives rise to distinct optical phenomena, such as inhibition of [spontaneous emission](/wiki/Spontaneous_emission "Spontaneous emission"),[[4]](#cite_note-Yablonovitch1987-4) high-reflecting omni-directional mirrors, and low-loss-[waveguiding](/wiki/Waveguide "Waveguide"). The bandgap of photonic crystals can be understood as the destructive [interference](/wiki/Wave_interference "Wave interference") of multiple reflections of light propagating in the crystal at each interface between layers of high- and low- refractive index regions, akin to the bandgaps of electrons in solids.

There are two strategies for opening up the complete photonic band gap. The first one is to increase the refractive index contrast for the band gap in each direction becomes wider and the second one is to make the [Brillouin zone](/wiki/Brillouin_zone "Brillouin zone") more similar to sphere.[[5]](#cite_note-Yablonovitch1991-5) However, the former is limited by the available technologies and materials and the latter is restricted by the [crystallographic restriction theorem](/wiki/Crystallographic_restriction_theorem "Crystallographic restriction theorem"). For this reason, the photonic crystals with a complete band gap demonstrated to date have [face-centered cubic](/wiki/Cubic_crystal_system "Cubic crystal system") lattice with the most spherical Brillouin zone and made of high-refractive-index semiconductor materials. Another approach is to exploit quasicrystalline structures with no crystallography limits. A complete photonic bandgap was reported for low-index polymer quasicrystalline samples manufactured by 3D printing.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The periodicity of the photonic crystal structure must be around or greater than half the wavelength (in the medium) of the light waves in order for interference effects to be exhibited. [Visible light](/wiki/Visible_light "Visible light") ranges in wavelength between about 400 nm (violet) to about 700 nm (red) and the resulting wavelength inside a material requires dividing that by the average [index of refraction](/wiki/Index_of_refraction "Index of refraction"). The repeating regions of high and low dielectric constant must, therefore, be fabricated at this scale. In one dimension, this is routinely accomplished using the techniques of [thin-film deposition](/wiki/Thin-film_deposition "Thin-film deposition").

## History

Photonic crystals have been studied in one form or another since 1887, but no one used the term *photonic crystal* until over 100 years later—after [Eli Yablonovitch](/wiki/Eli_Yablonovitch "Eli Yablonovitch") and [Sajeev John](/wiki/Sajeev_John "Sajeev John") published two milestone papers on photonic crystals in 1987.[[4]](#cite_note-Yablonovitch1987-4)[[7]](#cite_note-John1987-7) The early history is well-documented in the form of a story when it was identified as one of the landmark developments in physics by the [American Physical Society](/wiki/American_Physical_Society "American Physical Society").[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Before 1987, one-dimensional photonic crystals in the form of periodic multi-layer dielectric stacks (such as the [Bragg mirror](/wiki/Distributed_Bragg_reflector "Distributed Bragg reflector")) were studied extensively. [Lord Rayleigh](/wiki/Lord_Rayleigh "Lord Rayleigh") started their study in 1887,[[9]](#cite_note-Rayleigh1888-9) by showing that such systems have a one-dimensional photonic band-gap, a spectral range of large reflectivity, known as a *stop-band*. Today, such structures are used in a diverse range of applications—from reflective coatings to enhancing LED efficiency to highly reflective mirrors in certain laser cavities (see, for example, [VCSEL](/wiki/VCSEL "VCSEL")). The pass-bands and stop-bands in photonic crystals were first reduced to practice by [Melvin M. Weiner](/wiki/Melvin_M._Weiner "Melvin M. Weiner")[[10]](#cite_note-10) who called those crystals "discrete phase-ordered media." Weiner achieved those results by extending Darwin's[[11]](#cite_note-11) dynamical theory for x-ray Bragg diffraction to arbitrary wavelengths, angles of incidence, and cases where the incident wavefront at a lattice plane is scattered appreciably in the forward-scattered direction. A detailed theoretical study of one-dimensional optical structures was performed by [Vladimir P. Bykov](/w/index.php?title=Vladimir_P._Bykov&action=edit&redlink=1 "Vladimir P. Bykov (page does not exist)"),[[12]](#cite_note-Bykov1972-12) who was the first to investigate the effect of a photonic band-gap on the spontaneous emission from atoms and molecules embedded within the photonic structure. Bykov also speculated as to what could happen if two- or three-dimensional periodic optical structures were used.[[13]](#cite_note-Bykov1975-13) The concept of three-dimensional photonic crystals was then discussed by Ohtaka in 1979,[[14]](#cite_note-Ohtaka1979-14) who also developed a formalism for the calculation of the photonic band structure. However, these ideas did not take off until after the publication of two milestone papers in 1987 by Yablonovitch and John. Both these papers concerned high-dimensional periodic optical structures, i.e., photonic crystals. Yablonovitch's main goal was to engineer photonic [density of states](/wiki/Density_of_states "Density of states") to control the [spontaneous emission](/wiki/Spontaneous_emission "Spontaneous emission") of materials embedded in the photonic crystal. John's idea was to use photonic crystals to affect localisation and control of light.

After 1987, the number of research papers concerning photonic crystals began to grow exponentially. However, due to the difficulty of fabricating these structures at optical scales (see [Fabrication challenges](#Fabrication_challenges)), early studies were either theoretical or in the microwave regime, where photonic crystals can be built on the more accessible centimetre scale. (This fact is due to a property of the [electromagnetic fields](/wiki/Electromagnetic_fields "Electromagnetic fields") known as scale invariance. In essence, electromagnetic fields, as the solutions to [Maxwell's equations](/wiki/Maxwell%27s_equations "Maxwell's equations"), have no natural length scale—so solutions for centimetre scale structure at microwave frequencies are the same as for nanometre scale structures at optical frequencies.)

By 1991, Yablonovitch had demonstrated the first three-dimensional photonic band-gap in the microwave regime.[[5]](#cite_note-Yablonovitch1991-5) The structure that Yablonovitch was able to produce involved drilling an array of holes in a transparent material, where the holes of each layer form an inverse diamond structure – today it is known as [Yablonovite](/wiki/Yablonovite "Yablonovite").

In 1996, [Thomas Krauss](/wiki/Thomas_F_Krauss "Thomas F Krauss") demonstrated a two-dimensional photonic crystal at optical wavelengths.[[15]](#cite_note-Krauss1996-15) This opened the way to fabricate photonic crystals in semiconductor materials by borrowing methods from the semiconductor industry.

Pavel Cheben demonstrated a new type of photonic crystal waveguide – subwavelength grating (SWG) waveguide.[[16]](#cite_note-16)[[17]](#cite_note-:1-17) The SWG waveguide operates in subwavelength region, away from the bandgap. It allows the waveguide properties to be controlled directly by the nanoscale engineering of the resulting [metamaterial](/wiki/Metamaterial "Metamaterial") while mitigating wave interference effects. This provided “a missing degree of freedom in photonics”[[18]](#cite_note-18) and resolved an important limitation in [silicon photonics](/wiki/Silicon_photonics "Silicon photonics") which was its restricted set of available materials insufficient to achieve complex optical on-chip functions.[[19]](#cite_note-:2-19)[[20]](#cite_note-20)

Today, such techniques use photonic crystal slabs, which are two dimensional photonic crystals "etched" into slabs of semiconductor. [Total internal reflection](/wiki/Total_internal_reflection "Total internal reflection") confines light to the slab, and allows photonic crystal effects, such as engineering photonic dispersion in the slab. Researchers around the world are looking for ways to use photonic crystal slabs in integrated computer chips, to improve optical processing of communications—both on-chip and between chips.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Autocloning fabrication technique, proposed for [infrared](/wiki/Infrared "Infrared") and visible range photonic crystals by Sato et al. in 2002, uses [electron-beam lithography](/wiki/Electron-beam_lithography "Electron-beam lithography") and [dry etching](/wiki/Dry_etching "Dry etching"): lithographically formed layers of periodic grooves are stacked by regulated [sputter deposition](/wiki/Sputter_deposition "Sputter deposition") and etching, resulting in "stationary corrugations" and periodicity. [Titanium dioxide](/wiki/Titanium_dioxide "Titanium dioxide")/[silica](/wiki/Silicon_dioxide "Silicon dioxide") and [tantalum pentoxide](/wiki/Tantalum_pentoxide "Tantalum pentoxide")/silica devices were produced, exploiting their dispersion characteristics and suitability to sputter deposition.[[21]](#cite_note-autocloning-21)

Such techniques have yet to mature into commercial applications, but two-dimensional photonic crystals are commercially used in [photonic crystal fibres](/wiki/Photonic-crystal_fiber "Photonic-crystal fiber")[[22]](#cite_note-Ouellette2002-22) (otherwise known as holey fibres, because of the air holes that run through them). Photonic crystal fibres were first developed by [Philip Russell](/wiki/Philip_Russell_(physicist) "Philip Russell (physicist)") in 1998, and can be designed to possess enhanced properties over (normal) [optical fibres](/wiki/Optical_fibres "Optical fibres").

Study has proceeded more slowly in three-dimensional than in two-dimensional photonic crystals. This is because of more difficult fabrication.[[22]](#cite_note-Ouellette2002-22) Three-dimensional photonic crystal fabrication had no inheritable semiconductor industry techniques to draw on. Attempts have been made, however, to adapt some of the same techniques, and quite advanced examples have been demonstrated,[[23]](#cite_note-JohnsonMITL3-23) for example in the construction of "woodpile" structures constructed on a planar layer-by-layer basis. Another strand of research has tried to construct three-dimensional photonic structures from [self-assembly](/wiki/Self-assembly "Self-assembly")—essentially letting a mixture of dielectric [nanospheres](/wiki/Nanosphere "Nanosphere") settle from solution into three-dimensionally periodic structures that have photonic band-gaps. [Vasily Astratov](/wiki/Vasily_Astratov "Vasily Astratov")'s group from the [Ioffe Institute](/wiki/Ioffe_Institute "Ioffe Institute") realized in 1995 that natural and synthetic [opals](/wiki/Opal "Opal") are photonic crystals with an incomplete bandgap.[[24]](#cite_note-Ioffe-24) The first demonstration of an "inverse opal" structure with a complete photonic bandgap came in 2000, from researchers at the [University of Toronto](/wiki/University_of_Toronto "University of Toronto"), and Institute of Materials Science of Madrid (ICMM-CSIC), Spain.[[25]](#cite_note-Blanco2000-25) The ever-expanding field of natural photonics, [bioinspiration](/wiki/Bioinspiration "Bioinspiration") and [biomimetics](/wiki/Biomimicry "Biomimicry")—the study of natural structures to better understand and use them in design—is also helping researchers in photonic crystals.[[26]](#cite_note-Kinoshita2008-26)[[27]](#cite_note-Kolle2011-27)[[28]](#cite_note-McPhedran2015-28)[[29]](#cite_note-Mouchet2021-29) For example, in 2006 a naturally occurring photonic crystal was discovered in the scales of a Brazilian beetle.[[30]](#cite_note-Bartl2007-30) Analogously, in 2012 a diamond crystal structure was found in a weevil[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[32]](#cite_note-32) and a gyroid-type architecture in a butterfly.[[33]](#cite_note-33) More recently, gyroid photonic crystals have been found in the feather barbs of [blue-winged leafbirds](/wiki/Blue-winged_leafbird "Blue-winged leafbird") and are responsible for the bird's shimmery blue coloration.[[34]](#cite_note-34) Some publications suggest the feasibility of the complete photonic band gap in the visible range in photonic crystals with optically saturated media that can be implemented by using laser light as an external optical pump.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

### One-dimensional photonic crystals

To produce a one-dimensional photonic crystal, [thin film](/wiki/Thin_film "Thin film") layers of different dielectric constant may be periodically deposited on a surface which leads to a band gap in a particular propagation direction (such as normal to the surface). A [Bragg grating](/wiki/Bragg_grating "Bragg grating") is an example of this type of photonic crystal. One-dimensional photonic crystals can include layers of [non-linear](/wiki/Non-linear_optics "Non-linear optics") optical materials in which the non-linear behaviour is accentuated due to field enhancement at wavelengths near a so-called degenerate band edge. A one-dimensional photonic crystal can be implemented using repeated alternating layers of a [metamaterial](/wiki/Metamaterial "Metamaterial") and vacuum.[[37]](#cite_note-37) If the metamaterial is such that the relative [permittivity](/wiki/Permittivity "Permittivity") and [permeability](/wiki/Permeability_(electromagnetism) "Permeability (electromagnetism)") follow the same wavelength dependence, then the photonic crystal behaves identically for [TE and TM modes](/wiki/Distributed_Bragg_reflector#TE_and_TM_mode_reflectivity "Distributed Bragg reflector"), that is, for both *s* and *p* [polarizations](/wiki/Polarization_(waves) "Polarization (waves)") of light incident at an angle.

Recently, researchers fabricated a graphene-based Bragg grating (one-dimensional photonic crystal) and demonstrated that it supports excitation of surface electromagnetic waves in the periodic structure by using 633 nm He-Ne laser as the light source.[[38]](#cite_note-38) Besides, a novel type of one-dimensional graphene-dielectric photonic crystal has also been proposed. This structure can act as a far-IR filter and can support low-loss surface plasmons for waveguide and sensing applications.[[39]](#cite_note-39) 1D photonic crystals doped with bio-active metals (i.e. [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver")) have been also proposed as sensing devices for [bacterial](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria") contaminants.[[40]](#cite_note-40) Similar planar 1D photonic crystals made of polymers have been used to detect [volatile organic compounds](/wiki/Volatile_organic_compound "Volatile organic compound") vapors in atmosphere.[[41]](#cite_note-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)
In addition to solid-phase photonic crystals, some liquid crystals with defined ordering can demonstrate photonic color.[[43]](#cite_note-:0-43) For example, studies have shown several liquid crystals with short- or long-range one-dimensional positional ordering can form photonic structures.[[43]](#cite_note-:0-43)

### Two-dimensional photonic crystals

In two dimensions, holes may be drilled in a substrate that is transparent to the wavelength of radiation that the bandgap is designed to block. Triangular and square lattices of holes have been successfully employed.

The **Holey fiber** or [photonic crystal fiber](/wiki/Photonic_crystal_fiber "Photonic crystal fiber") can be made by taking cylindrical rods of glass in hexagonal lattice, and then heating and stretching them, the triangle-like airgaps between the glass rods become the holes that confine the modes.

### Photonic crystal cavities

Not only band gap, photonic crystals may have another effect if we partially remove the symmetry through the creation a nanosize [cavity](/wiki/Optical_cavity "Optical cavity"). This defect allows you to guide or to trap the light with the same function as [nanophotonic resonator](/wiki/Nanophotonic_resonator "Nanophotonic resonator") and it is characterized by the strong dielectric modulation in the photonic crystals.[[50]](#cite_note-Joannopoulos-50) For the waveguide, the propagation of light depends on the in-plane control provided by the photonic band gap and to the long confinement of light induced by dielectric mismatch. For the light trap, the light is strongly confined in the cavity resulting further interactions with the materials. First, if we put a pulse of light inside the cavity, it will be delayed by nano- or picoseconds and this is proportional to the [quality factor](/wiki/Quality_factor "Quality factor") of the cavity. Finally, if we put an emitter inside the cavity, the emission light also can be enhanced significantly and or even the resonant coupling can go through Rabi oscillation. This is related with [cavity quantum electrodynamics](/wiki/Cavity_quantum_electrodynamics "Cavity quantum electrodynamics") and the interactions are defined by the weak and strong coupling of the emitter and the cavity. The first studies for the cavity in one-dimensional photonic slabs are usually in [grating](/wiki/Grating "Grating")[[51]](#cite_note-51) or [distributed feedback](/wiki/Distributed_feedback_laser "Distributed feedback laser") structures.[[52]](#cite_note-52) For two-dimensional photonic crystal cavities,[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) they are useful to make efficient photonic devices in telecommunication applications as they can provide very high quality factor up to millions with smaller-than-wavelength [mode volume](/wiki/Mode_volume "Mode volume"). For three-dimensional photonic crystal cavities, several methods have been developed including lithographic layer-by-layer approach,[[56]](#cite_note-56) surface [ion beam lithography](/wiki/Ion_beam_lithography "Ion beam lithography"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) and [micromanipulation](/wiki/Micromanipulator "Micromanipulator") technique.[[58]](#cite_note-58) All those mentioned photonic crystal cavities that tightly confine light offer very useful functionality for integrated photonic circuits, but it is challenging to produce them in a manner that allows them to be easily relocated.[[59]](#cite_note-59) There is no full control
with the cavity creation, the cavity location, and the emitter position relative to the maximum field of the cavity while the studies to solve those problems are still ongoing. Movable cavity of nanowire in photonic crystals is one of solutions to tailor this light matter interaction.[[60]](#cite_note-60)

## Fabrication challenges

Higher-dimensional photonic crystal fabrication faces two major challenges:

One promising fabrication method for two-dimensionally periodic photonic crystals is a photonic-crystal fiber, such as a *holey fiber*. Using fiber draw techniques developed for [communications fiber](/wiki/Optical_fiber "Optical fiber") it meets these two requirements, and photonic crystal fibres are commercially available. Another promising method for developing two-dimensional photonic crystals is the so-called photonic crystal slab. These structures consist of a slab of material—such as [silicon](/wiki/Silicon "Silicon")—that can be patterned using techniques from the semiconductor industry. Such chips offer the potential to combine photonic processing with electronic processing on a single chip.

For three dimensional photonic crystals, various techniques have been used—including [photolithography](/wiki/Photolithography "Photolithography") and etching techniques similar to those used for [integrated circuits](/wiki/Integrated_circuits "Integrated circuits").[[23]](#cite_note-JohnsonMITL3-23) Some of these techniques are already commercially available. To avoid the complex machinery of [nanotechnological methods](/wiki/Nanotechnology "Nanotechnology"), some alternate approaches involve growing photonic crystals from [colloidal crystals](/wiki/Colloidal_crystal "Colloidal crystal") as self-assembled structures.

Mass-scale 3D photonic crystal films and fibres can now be produced using a shear-assembly technique that stacks 200–300 nm colloidal polymer spheres into perfect films of [fcc](/wiki/Face-centered_cubic "Face-centered cubic") lattice. Because the particles have a softer transparent rubber coating, the films can be stretched and molded, tuning the photonic bandgaps and producing striking structural [color](/wiki/Color "Color") effects.

The various lines in the band structure, correspond to the different cases of *n*, the band index. For an introduction to photonic band structure, see K. Photonic crystals are optical media containing a periodic nanostructure – typically, a periodic variation of the [refractive index](refractive_index.html), where the occurring periods are of the order of the optical [wavelength](wavelength.html). While in some cases there is a quite large refractive index contrast, for example between a glass or a [semiconductor](semiconductors.html) and air, much lower refractive index contrasts occur in other cases. Some of the particularly remarkable photonic crystal properties – for example complete photonic band gaps – arise primarily in cases with large index contrast, and this is why some authors suggest to demand a large index contrast for photonic crystals.

This area of research, which is highly important both in terms of fundamental science and practical applications, has been pioneered by Eli Yablonovitch [[1]](#ref1 "E. Yablonovitch, Inhibited spontaneous emission in solid-state physics and electronics, Phys. Rev. Lett. 58 (20), 2059 (1987)") (who also introduced the term *photonic crystals*) and Sajeev John [[2]](#ref2 "S. John, Strong localization of photons in certain disordered dielectric superlattices, Phys. Rev. Lett. 58 (23), 2486 (1987) https://doi.org/10.1103/PhysRevLett.58.2486"), and followed by a large number of other researchers. Early research on one-dimensional periodic structures (multilayer [dielectric](dielectric_materials.html) stacks → *[dielectric coatings](dielectric_coatings.html)* as [Bragg mirrors](bragg_mirrors.html)) has already been performed by Lord Rayleigh in 1887. While photonic crystals are mostly dealt with in areas of modern technology, photonic crystal structures have also been observed in nature, for example and certain minerals (e.g. opal) and in living organisms, e.g. in butterfly wings.

There is an obvious similarity between photonic crystals and [photonic metamaterials](photonic_metamaterials.html): the latter also usually involve a periodic arrangement of structures with optical effects. However, photonic metamaterials have a structuring on a sub-wavelength scale, such that those structures appear like being homogeneous for optical fields, and the resulting remarkable properties are not explained with photonic band structures, but essentially with unusual values of the [refractive index](refractive_index.html).

## Analogy With Electrons in Crystalline Media

### Electron States and Bloch Functions

In the early 20th century, many of the fundamental properties of solids such as their widely varying electrical and thermal conductivity, optical transmissivity or [absorption](absorption.html) were still not understood at all. Substantial progress was then achieved by considering the wave functions of electrons in periodic electrostatic potentials, as are created by the periodic arrangements of atomic nuclei in a crystalline material.

While initially it had to be expected that the very strongly varying electric potentials should lead to strong scattering of electrons in such structures, it was discovered that such periodic structures exhibit eigenstates for electrons which can be described with *Bloch functions*. A Bloch function has the form

where ($k$) is a variable [wave vector](wave_vector.html), determining the phase evolution over long distances, and the ($u$) function has the periodicity of the electric potential. A Bloch wave is a standing wave – note that the intensity profile, determined by the ($u$) function, remains fixed in space. Different solutions are distinguished with a discrete index ($j$). The wave vector can in principle be chosen arbitrarily, but it is sufficient to consider only wave vectors inside the first Brillouin zone; vectors outside that zone only lead to the same solutions. With suitable mathematical methods, for a given potential one can calculate the resulting Bloch functions, and for each one the corresponding electron energy (as the eigenvalue). Considering an infinitely large crystal, one obtains a continuum of Bloch functions and energy values.

For each value of the index ($j$), the variation of the wave vector leads to a certain range of electron energies, which is called an *energy band*. These bands often overlap with each other, leading to wide continuous ranges of possible electron energies, but in certain situations [band gaps](band_gap.html) occur, i.e., ranges of electron energies which do not occur for any of the available eigenstates.

Each Bloch state is completely delocalized, meaning that an electron in such a state is not corresponding to a certain atomic nucleus, but can in principle be found anywhere in the whole crystal. Localized electronic states only arise for certain lattice defects.

An electron would in principle stay in its eigenstate forever, unless additional effects cause transitions between the states – either inside a band or across energy bands. Examples are [spontaneous emission](spontaneous_emission.html), [absorption](absorption.html) of [light](light.html) and [stimulated emission](stimulated_emission.html), also interactions with phonons, with other electrons or with lattice imperfections.

Without such additional coupling effects, the calculated states are eigenstates despite the strong scattering at the atomic nuclei. One consider the eigenstates as superpositions of a set of plane waves, moving in very different directions, such that despite the strong coupling between those their overall pattern remains stationary.

The resulting structure of the electron eigenstates in conjunction with Pauli's principle has various important consequences:

Many essential properties of insulators ([dielectrics](dielectric_materials.html)), semiconductors and metals can be explained based on their electronic band structures.

### Photon States in Periodic Media

In [photonics](photonics.html), one deals with the propagation of [light](light.html), i.e., electromagnetic waves. In the case of a periodic photonic nanostructure, described by a periodically varying [refractive index](refractive_index.html) ($n$) or dielectric constant ($\epsilon$), one can set up the wave equation under such conditions, where [plane waves](plane_waves.html) are of course not eigensolutions (as in a homogeneous medium).

Just as in solid-state physics for electrons, one can show that there are again Bloch waves, but this time with functions describing the complex electric field amplitude instead of electron wavefunctions. However, in various respects the circumstances are different:

Still, substantial parts of the insight from solid-state physics can be used in quite similar form for the analysis of photonic crystals:

In cases where the existence of a photonic band gap is essential, such materials are also often called *photonic bandgap materials*. They are also sometimes called *photonic insulators* or *photonic semiconductors* because electric insulators and semiconductors are those where the electronic band structure exhibits band gaps.

One may wonder whether the periodicity on the atomic scale (for crystalline materials) could also play a role. However, that length scale is much too short to be relevant for light, having wavelengths on a much longer scale.

## Photonic Crystals in One, Two and Three Dimensions

Photonic crystals can be realized in different dimensions, which are discussed in the following sections.

### One-dimensional Photonic Crystals

It is debatable whether one-dimensional periodic structures should be qualified as photonic crystals; the pioneer Yablonovitch rejected that. However, in any case they are a good starting point for analyzing fundamental properties of photonic crystals without additional complications which come into play for higher dimensions.

![1D structure](img/pcr_1d.png)

As mentioned above, one-dimensional periodic structures in the form of dielectric multilayer stacks (Figure 1) have already been considered in the late 19th century by Lord Rayleigh. Nowadays, they are routinely used as [dielectric mirrors](dielectric_mirrors.html), in the simplest case with a design of a [Bragg mirror](bragg_mirrors.html). One frequently just ignores the additional spatial dimensions by considering only light propagating into exactly opposite directions, coupled to each other by the periodic refractive index modulation.

The mathematically simplest case actually occurs for a sinusoidal (rather than rectangular) modulation of the dielectric constant, although for technical applications the step-wise modulation of a laminated material is much more common – realized by using layers of two different materials, rather than a continuous modulation as used in the much less common [rugate filters](rugate_filters.html). It can be shown quite easily that for a vanishing amplitude of the modulation of dielectric constant one obtains a simple band structure without any band gaps, but band gaps of increasing size then arise for an increasing strength of the modulation. In such frequency regions, one obtains an exponential decay of electric field amplitude in one direction (ignoring the modulation on short length scales), meaning that light cannot propagate over substantial distances into such a structure.

![1D photonic bandgap](img/photonic_bandgap_1d.png)

By calculating the optical properties of a Bragg mirror with 100 layer pairs, one can already explore to some extent the approximate properties of a photonic bandgap of an infinite periodic structure, extending in ($z$) direction from zero to infinity. Figure 2 shows the field penetration into such a structure. Within the wavelength range from approximately 875 nm to 1165 nm, one obtains strong reflection and penetration of the optical field into the structure only to a quite limited extent (a few microns). Light outside that wavelength range can propagate deep into the structure. It is only that the intensity pattern in that “propagating region” would still get finer and finer with an increasing number of layer pairs; of course, one does not get the true band structure of the infinite photonic crystal with that type of calculation. (Anyway, existing structures are always finite.) However, a more and more increasing number of layer pairs does not have a substantial influence on the width of the reflecting wavelength range; that is essentially determined by the refractive index contrast (in the example case between titanium dioxide and [silica](fused_silica.html)). ### Two-dimensional Photonic Crystals

Two-dimensional photonic crystal structures are understood to be periodic in two spatial dimensions, while there is no modulation of dielectric constant in the third dimension. For example, one can consider a hexagonal structure, where Figure 3 shows a cross-section (which would look exactly the same for all ($z$) values, assuming that the ($z$) direction is perpendicular to the drawing plane). The air holes (shown in white) run through the structure infinitely in ($z$) direction.

![2D photonic crystal](img/photonic_crystal_2d.png)

Figure 3 shows as an example a hexagonal structure, which belongs to the particularly common ones. One may also invert such a structure, i.e., use a pattern of cylinders surrounded by voids, and held in position either by very thin strands (with negligible optical effects) or with two solid planes at the edges of a device.

Again, one may ignore the possibility that the ($k$) vector could have a component in the ($z$) direction. That would be appropriate for a situation where one tries to inject only such kind of light into the structure. However, such a situation is rarely found in practical applications. It is more common that one realizes a relatively flat 2D photonic crystal (a *photonic crystal slab*) which is enclosed on two sides e.g. with a homogeneous material of substantially lower refractive index, confining the light in that dimension with the classical [waveguide](waveguides.html) approach. At least light hitting such an interface with a large enough angle of incidence, one obtains [total internal reflection](total_internal_reflection.html). If the photonic crystal slab is thin enough, it can have [single-mode](single_mode_fibers.html) properties in the transverse dimension.

Another common situation is that the considered light even more or less propagates in the ($z$) direction. That is the case for [photonic crystal fibers](photonic_crystal_fibers.html) – which, however, also require an additional lattice defect for obtaining a [waveguide](waveguides.html) – to be discussed further below.

An important point to emphasize is that it is not only more difficult to calculate band structures of 2D photonic crystals, but also more difficult to find designs which exhibit complete band gaps in the sense that within a certain range of optical frequencies there is no direction at all in which light can propagate without an exponential decay of amplitude. That is particularly the case when different polarization directions are also considered. It can easily happen, for example, that one obtains a band gap only for TE polarization, but not for TM polarization, or vice versa. Only with carefully chosen structures and a relatively large refractive index contrast (which precludes calculations with purely scalar field models), complete band gaps are possible. Such difficulties help one to understand that it took about a century from first thoughts on one-dimensional structures to a good understanding of two-dimensional photonic crystals.

Various technologies have been developed for the fabrication of 2D photonic crystal structures. Compared with 3D structures (see below), this is substantially simpler, basically because one has good access to the whole structure from one side and may later on deposit additional homogeneous material, if not simply using an interface to air. For an example for such technology, see the article on [photonic crystal surface-emitting lasers](photonic_crystal_surface_emitting_lasers.html). Often, one utilizes technologies which have been well developed in the context of semiconductor processing for [optoelectronics](optoelectronics.html).

### Three-dimensional Photonic Crystals

It is easy to imagine various kinds of 3D photonic crystal structures, for example

3D photonic crystals are generally more challenging to fabricate, as is fairly obvious particularly because of the tiny dimensions dictated by the short optical wavelengths, and the resulting extreme demands in terms of resolution and accuracy:

After the pioneering work on 2D photonic crystals, it took only a couple of years to find the first 3D photonic crystal structures with complete band gaps, and to fabricate them. The first one contained dielectric spheres arranged in the diamond structure [[4]](#ref4 "K. M. Ho, C. T. Chan and C. M. Soukoulis, Existence of a photonic gap in periodic dielectric structures, Phys. Rev. Lett. 65 (25), 3152 (1990)"). A previous report concerning a face-centered cubic (fcc) structure [[3]](#ref3 "E. Yablonovitch and T. J. Gmitter, Photonic band structure: the face-centered cubic case, Phys. Rev. Lett. 63 (18), 1950 (1989)") had to be revised. It turned out that fcc structures usually exhibit a semimetallic band structure (i.e., not with real band gaps), although a complete band gap is possible for a structure with very large refractive index contrast of greater than 3:1 [[5]](#ref5 "E. Yablonovitch and T. J. Gmitter, Photonic band structure: the face-centered cubic case, J. Opt. Soc. Am. A 7 (9), 1792 (1990)"). Another possibility is the “woodpile” structure, which was first demonstrated for microwave and mid-infrared frequencies, later also for the near infrared including the telecom wavelength region around 1.5 μm [[20](#ref20 "J. G. Fleming and S. Y. Lin, Three-dimensional photonic crystal with a stop band from 1.35 to 1.95 μm, Opt. Lett. 24 (1), 49 (1999)"), [24](#ref24 "S. Noda et al., Full three-dimensional photonic bandgap crystals at near-infrared wavelengths, Science 289 (5479), 604 (2000)")]. With a 3D tungsten photonic crystal structure, it became possible to substantially control the emission of [thermal radiation](thermal_radiation.html) such that it is strong for wavelength around 1.5 μm while it is suppressed for long wavelength beyond 3 μm.

## Omnidirectional Reflection; Lattice Defects

We have seen that a photonic band gap essentially prohibits the propagation of light in the medium within a certain optical frequency range. This allows a photonic crystal to act as a kind of *omnidirectional reflector*, assuming that the band gap exists for all relevant propagation directions – ideally, for any directions in three dimensions. A photonic crystal material is thus suitable for enclosing structures to prevent light from escaping. Indeed, the pioneering paper of Yablonovitch in 1987 [[1]](#ref1 "E. Yablonovitch, Inhibited spontaneous emission in solid-state physics and electronics, Phys. Rev. Lett. 58 (20), 2059 (1987)") already described the use of a three-dimensional photonic crystal for suppressing [spontaneous emission](spontaneous_emission.html) of excited atoms or ions inside the medium. It had already been known from [quantum optics](quantum_optics.html) that the possibility of emission of radiation is subject to the availability of suitable modes of the light field, and a photonic crystal can thus suppress spontaneous emission by removing such propagation modes.

Similarly, a photonic crystal can be used to confine light to different kinds of structures, which can be considered as some kind of intentionally introduced lattice defects. Those provide localized defect states within a photonic bandgap [[9]](#ref9 "R. D. Meade et al., Novel applications of photonic bandgap materials: Low-loss bands and high Q cavities, J. Appl. Phys. 75, 4753 (1994)"):

![2D photonic crystal with lattice defects](img/photonic_crystal_2d2.png)

The concept of intentionally introducing certain lattice defects, e.g. for resonators and waveguides, and also to integrated light-emitting features, is also applicable in three dimensions. Only, it is again quite difficult to implement, and also to optimally design such structures. Propagation losses of waveguides resulting from imperfections need to be considered [[33]](#ref33 "C. Sell et al., Propagation and loss in three-dimensional photonic crystal waveguides with imperfect confinement, Phys. Rev. B. 68, 113106 (2003)"). Nevertheless, there are some results:

## Computational Methods

A detailed analysis of band structures of photonic crystal materials and related devices is of crucial importance not only for acquiring a good quantitative understanding of such structures and devices, but also for successful design optimization. It requires quite sophisticated mathematical and computational tools – particularly if a comprehensive 3D model is required. As was pointed out already, a full vectorial description of the electromagnetic field is usually needed due to the necessary large refractive index contrast. Rather different techniques have been developed, which can have a specific advantages (e.g. accuracy and computational efficiency) but also serious limitations such as applicability only to specific cases:

A particularly difficult area is the comprehensive study of the effects of 3D photonic crystal structures with lattice defects and random imperfections, as can result from deficiencies of fabrication methods. Even on modern computers, such computations can be fairly demanding in terms of computation time and required computer memory.

Because of the crucial importance of such computation methods and their difficulty, the progress of the scientific research and practical applications substantially depends on advances in that field.

## Applications of Photonic Crystal Structures

In the previous paragraphs, the focus was on the physical foundations, but some possible applications have already been mentioned. Indeed, a wide range of applications is at least conceivable, although only a small part has been demonstrated to be suitable already for widespread practical use. In the following, a brief summary of possible applications is given.

### Quantum Optics Research

The substantial change in the electromagnetic mode structure caused by a photonic crystal has profound implications on [spontaneous emission](spontaneous_emission.html). That can be explained in [quantum optics](quantum_optics.html), and is the basis for the use of photonic crystals in various quantum optics experiments for fundamental research. However, there are also various practical uses, as explained in the following sections.

### Single-mode LEDs

A [light-emitting diode](light_emitting_diodes.html) usually emits into a very large number of radiation modes, i.e., with basically no spatial [coherence](coherence.html). That can be changed, however, by strongly modifying the mode structure of the radiation field with a photonic crystal. In the extreme case, one obtains single-mode LEDs, which emit most of the output power into a single mode. That allows efficient coupling into a single-mode waveguide. Such devices also allow very high modulation frequencies [[51]](#ref51 "G. Shambat et al., Ultrafast direct modulation of a single-mode photonic crystal nanocavity light-emitting diode, Nature Communications 2, 539 (2011)"), which is important for applications in communications, for example. Such single-mode LEDs may thus compete with [VCSELs](vertical_cavity_surface_emitting_lasers.html), for example.

### Photonic Integrated Circuits

For application in [optical fiber communications](optical_fiber_communications.html), [optical metrology](optical_metrology.html) and other areas, the prospect of obtaining powerful [photonic integrated circuits](photonic_integrated_circuits.html) has been a substantial driver of research for many years, although it has turned out to be quite difficult to fabricate well working devices at reasonable cost.

Typically, photonic band gaps are exploited for confining light to resonators or waveguides, which may be coupled to each other. A substantial amount of work has focused on 2D photonic crystals, mainly because those are substantially easier to fabricate than 3D structures, and sufficient for many purposes.
3D photonic chips, however, would offer a substantially increased potential for implementing complex functionalities in rather small volumes.

A range of physical effects beyond the formation of waveguides in resonators might be exploited. Some examples:

### Photonic Crystal Fibers

[Photonic crystal fibers](photonic_crystal_fibers.html) [[37]](#ref37 "P. St. J. Russell, Photonic crystal fibers (review paper), Science 299, 358 (2003)") are an example for two-dimensional photonic crystal structures, where light propagation is dominantly in directions which are approximately perpendicular to the photonic crystal plane.
Typically, one has some regular pattern of air holes with sub-micrometer diameters running through the whole length of a fiber. A common design carries a missing hole in the center, which forms the fiber waveguide. However, there are also many more sophisticated designs – for example, of [hollow-core fibers](hollow_core_fibers.html), which partly rely on photonic bandgaps.

Note that not all microstructure fibers are truly photonic crystal fibers because some of them lack an actual crystal symmetry.

See the article on [photonic crystal fibers](photonic_crystal_fibers.html) for more details.

### Photonic Crystal Surface-emitting Lasers

There is a type of [semiconductor lasers](semiconductor_lasers.html), called *photonic crystal surface-emitting lasers*, where the gain region is a two-dimensional photonic crystal structure [[22](#ref22 "M. Imada et al., Coherent two-dimensional lasing action in surface-emitting laser with triangular-lattice photonic crystal structure, Appl. Phys. Lett. 75 (3), 316 (1999)"), [28](#ref28 "S. Noda et al., Polarization mode control of two-dimensional photonic crystal laser by a unit cell structure design, Science 293 (5532), 1123 (2001)")]. This helps to ensure emission with high spatial [coherence](coherence.html) despite a large emitting area. As a consequence of that, one can achieve very high [beam quality](beam_quality.html) in combination with high output power levels.

See the article on [photonic crystal surface-emitting lasers](photonic_crystal_surface_emitting_lasers.html) for more details.

### Control of Thermal Emission

Thermal emission has been applied for a very long time for light generation in [incandescent lamps](incandescent_lamps.html). A fundamental problem has always been that the spectral shape of the emission can be controlled only to a quite limited extent through the operation temperature; the largest part of the emitted power is in unusable [infrared](infrared_light.html) spectral region. enriched_points.expanded_content: Photonic crystals are engineered materials with a periodic dielectric structure, manipulating light propagation similarly to how semiconductors influence electron flow.
They possess photonic band gaps, preventing certain light wavelengths from propagating, enabling precise control over light behavior, essential for advanced optical applications.
Photonic crystals are pivotal in telecommunications, lasers, sensors, and energy systems, enhancing efficiencies in optical devices and enabling innovations in integrated photonics.
